summary:
The volcanic eruption of Thera, the island now known as Santorini in the southern Aegean, was one of the largest volcanic events of the past four thousand years and had far-reaching consequences for Bronze Age Mediterranean civilizations. Scientific dating places the eruption most likely around 1628 BC, though debate about the precise date continues; the event deposited a thick layer of ash across a wide area of the eastern Mediterranean and Aegean, measurable in ice cores from Greenland and the Arctic. The eruption followed a Plinian column of ash and pyroclastic flows that collapsed the central caldera of the island.

The town of Akrotiri on the southern coast of Thera preserves an extraordinarily intact picture of Aegean Bronze Age life. Buried under volcanic debris in a fashion resembling Pompeii, the site reveals multi-story buildings with sophisticated drainage systems, elaborate painted frescoes depicting landscapes, animals, and human figures, and large storage vessels indicating a prosperous mercantile community closely connected to [[event:53|Minoan Crete]]. Notably, no human remains have been found at Akrotiri, suggesting that the inhabitants received adequate warning and evacuated before the final catastrophic phase of the eruption.

The relationship between the Thera eruption and the eventual decline of [[event:53|Minoan civilization]] around 1450 BC has been extensively debated. The immediate effects — tsunamis, ash fall on agricultural land, disruption of trade — would have been severe for Cretan coastal communities, but whether these effects were catastrophic enough to destabilize Minoan palace society remains uncertain. The eruption has also been linked in popular writing to the Atlantis legend recorded by Plato, though the chronological and geographical discrepancies between the two make this connection highly speculative.
